questions.q: What is domain authority and how is it calculated?
options: A metric measuring how old a domain is
A Moz metric predicting ranking strength based on backlink profile and quality
The number of pages indexed on a domain
A Google metric directly determining page rank
questions.explanation: Domain Authority (DA) by Moz predicts a site's ability to rank based on link profile quality and quantity on a 0-100 scale. It's not Google's official metric (that's indirectly measured via rankings); age and page count alone don't determine it.

questions.q: Why should you avoid buying backlinks as a link-building strategy?
options: Buying links is always free anyway
Google considers paid links a violation of guidelines and may apply manual penalties
Paid links automatically get you ranking benefits
There's no risk to buying links from low-quality sites
questions.explanation: Purchased links violate Google's Webmaster Guidelines and can result in ranking drops or de-indexing if detected. Google actively penalizes artificial link schemes; natural, earned links carry far more weight and sustainable value.

questions.q: What does 'nofollow' attribute on a link tell search engines?
options: Don't follow the link at all, ever
Ignore this link for ranking purposes, don't pass authority
The link is broken or invalid
Only Google should follow it, not other crawlers
questions.explanation: Nofollow signals that the link shouldn't pass ranking credit or authority. Google still crawls nofollow links for discovery but doesn't consolidate link equity through them; they're useful for user-generated content and affiliate links.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between 'link quality' and 'link quantity' in rankings?
options: Quality doesn't matter; quantity is everything
One high-quality link from a relevant authority site often outweighs many low-quality links
Quantity always beats quality no matter what
Both quality and quantity are irrelevant for modern rankings
questions.explanation: A single relevant, authoritative backlink often delivers more ranking power than hundreds of low-quality ones. Over-optimization through quantity (link farms, directory submissions) now triggers penalties; modern algorithms reward editorial quality and topical relevance.

questions.q: What role does anchor text play in communicating link relevance?
options: Anchor text has no SEO impact whatsoever
Over-optimized exact-match anchor text can trigger unnatural link warnings
Anchor text should always be the target keyword repeated
Search engines ignore anchor text completely
questions.explanation: Anchor text provides context about the linked content but over-optimization (excessive exact-match keywords) flags links as unnatural. Varied, natural anchor text (branded, generic, partial match) looks earned; overuse of perfect-match keywords is a red flag for algorithms.

questions.q: How do topical backlinks differ from generic backlinks in SEO value?
options: There is no difference; all backlinks have equal weight
Topically relevant links from related industry sites signal stronger authority in that niche
Generic links are always more valuable than topical ones
Only .edu and .gov domains provide topical relevance
questions.explanation: Topically relevant backlinks from related sites carry more ranking weight than random links because they signal genuine credibility in a specific field. Google's algorithm factors in link context and site relationship; a finance link to a tech site is weaker than a tech-to-tech link.

questions.q: What is the relationship between link building and brand reputation?
options: Link building has no connection to brand reputation
Earning links through quality content and reputation naturally builds authority and trust
Only paid ads can build brand reputation
Brand reputation has nothing to do with search rankings
questions.explanation: Legitimate link building stems from strong content and brand reputation; sites respected by industry earn more links naturally. This reputation signals translate to both user trust and search algorithm signals; paid ads build visibility but not earned link authority.

questions.q: How does internal linking structure affect link equity distribution on your site?
options: Internal links don't affect link equity at all
Strategic internal links distribute authority to priority pages and establish information architecture
You should link every page to every other page equally
Internal links dilute authority and should be minimized
questions.explanation: Internal linking passes authority strategically; linking to priority pages concentrates equity where it matters. Excessive linking wastes juice across unimportant pages; minimal linking leaves authority untapped; thoughtful architecture balances both.

questions.q: What is a 'broken backlink' opportunity in link building?
options: A broken backlink means the link is spammy and should be ignored
When a competitor's linked page is removed, you can pitch your content as a replacement
Broken links automatically harm your rankings
You should create broken links intentionally to gain attention
questions.explanation: Broken link building identifies when valuable pages linking to competitors disappear, then pitch your similar content as a replacement. This creates natural editorial links; it's a legitimate earned strategy—not spam—because it solves a real problem for webmasters.

questions.q: Why might a website avoid exchanging reciprocal links as a link-building tactic?
options: Reciprocal links always boost rankings equally
Excessive reciprocal link exchanges can appear unnatural and trigger penalty filters
Reciprocal links are the most natural form of linking
Google has never penalized reciprocal linking
questions.explanation: Widespread reciprocal link exchanges (link wheels, mutual link swaps) look artificial and can trigger unnatural link warnings. Occasional reciprocal links between related sites are normal; systematic schemes are flagged as manipulative.
